coredata.dc:title: UV-induced peroxidase-like activity of gold nanoclusters for differentiating pathogenic bacteria and detection of enterotoxin with colorimetric readout
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Xiaoxue
Tan, Fang
Xu, Aiqing
Deng, Keqin
Zeng, Yulong
Huang, Haowen
coredata.dc:description: In this study, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) in the presence of chitosan were prepared assisted by UV irradiation, which exhibit peroxidase-like property tested with H2O2/3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). The chitosan-AuNCs composite membrane also behaves peroxidase-like property and was characterized by TEM, AFM and SEM, which reveal the formed membrane was homogeneous and the AuNCs are predominantly spherical and well-dispersed without agglomeration in the membrane. A colorimetric assay was established by integrating immunoreactions occurred on the enzyme mimicking AuNC-chitosan composite membrane with varying colored GNPs serving as output signal. Three kinds of bacteria were chosen to serve as model bacteria to evaluate the selectivity of this sensor and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), can be easily differentiated from Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis). Such a colorimetric sensing is an extendable strategy due to the recognition element of probe may be replaceable on the catalytic AuNC-chitosan composite membrane. Staphylococcal enterotoxin B (SE-B) as low as 1.0 × 10−12 g/mL may be sensitively detected by naked-eye readout. As a practical application, the developed colorimetric strategy was used for rapid onsite screening strategy for S. aureus and detecting SE-B in food samples. This colorimetric strategy provides promising platform for rapid onsite screening pathogenic bacteria and detection of enterotoxin in food safety and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AuNC-chitosan composite membrane
Peroxidase-like property
Colorimetric assay
Pathogenic bacteria
Enterotoxin

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of peroxidase-like AuNCs by UV irradiation
Preparation of peroxidase-like AuNC-Chitosan composite membrane
Fabrication colorimetric sensor based AuNC-chitosan composite membrane
Differentiating bacteria and detection of staphylococcal enterotoxin B

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of AuNC-chitosan composite membrane based biosensor.

a: UV–vis absorption (black line) and photoemission (red line, under excitation at 320 nm) spectra of AuNCs. b: TEM images of the AuNCs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

a: Fluorescent variation of AuNCs irradiated by UV at time intervals, and the corresponding color change of the resulting AuNCs (b). c: Absorption spectrum after AuNCs irradiated by UV. d: TEM image of UV-irradiated AuNCs.

(a) Photographs for H2O2 and TMB (i), H2O2 and TMB reaction in the presence of initial AuNCs (ii) and UV-irradiarted AuNCs (ii). (b) Color gradient obtained from H2O2, TMB and AuNCs irradiated by UV at time intervals.
a: TEM image of chitosan membrane, and AuNC-chitosan composite membrane characterized by SEM (b) and AFM (c). d: TEM image of UV-irradiated AuNCs dispersed in chitosan membrane.

a: Test of proxidase-like property of chitosan membrane and AuNC-chitosan composite membrane in the presence of TMB and H2O2. b: The effect of thickness of AuNC-chitosan membrane made by different concentrations of chitosan on its peroxidase-like activity in the presence of TMB and H2O2.

a: Absorbance of the GNPs acquired from the reaction of HAuCl4 and H2O2 in the presence of AuNCs before and after UV irradiation at time intervals. b: Colored GNPs obtained from the reaction of HAuCl4 and H2O2 in the presence of AuNCs before and after UV irradiation.
Colorimetric readout for differentiating bacteria. b: Colorimetric detection of SEB by the colorimetric assay.

Detection of SE-B by the colorimetric assay. a: Photograph of the detection of SEB. b: Calibration curve of the difference of absorbance vs concentration of SE-B. A0 is the absorbance of GNPs in control experiment; A1 is the absorbance of GNPs generated after the addition of SE-B. Error bars indicate the standard deviation of three independent measurements. c: Photograph of the detection of food samples spiked with SE-B and (d) presents the results measured by the proposed colorimetric assay.

head.ce:title: UV-induced peroxidase-like activity of gold nanoclusters for differentiating pathogenic bacteria and detection of enterotoxin with colorimetric readout

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Function Molecule, Ministry of Education, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Controllable Preparation and Functional Application of Fine Polymers, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, 411201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Function Molecule
Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Controllable Preparation and Functional Application of Fine Polymers
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, 411201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) in the presence of chitosan were prepared assisted by UV irradiation, which exhibit peroxidase-like property tested with H2O2/3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). The chitosan-AuNCs composite membrane also behaves peroxidase-like property and was characterized by TEM, AFM and SEM, which reveal the formed membrane was homogeneous and the AuNCs are predominantly spherical and well-dispersed without agglomeration in the membrane. A colorimetric assay was established by integrating immunoreactions occurred on the enzyme mimicking AuNC-chitosan composite membrane with varying colored GNPs serving as output signal. Three kinds of bacteria were chosen to serve as model bacteria to evaluate the selectivity of this sensor and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), can be easily differentiated from Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis). Such a colorimetric sensing is an extendable strategy due to the recognition element of probe may be replaceable on the catalytic AuNC-chitosan composite membrane. Staphylococcal enterotoxin B (SE-B) as low as 1.0 × 10−12 g/mL may be sensitively detected by naked-eye readout. As a practical application, the developed colorimetric strategy was used for rapid onsite screening strategy for S. aureus and detecting SE-B in food samples. This colorimetric strategy provides promising platform for rapid onsite screening pathogenic bacteria and detection of enterotoxin in food safety and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne diseases caused by microbial contamination become world public health issues [1]. S. aures, a widespread pathogenic microorganism widely distributed in nature, is one of the important pathogens causing bacterial food poisoning [2,3]. The pathogenicity mainly depends on the toxins secreted by the bacteria. It has reported that many methods such as microbiological techniques [4], immunological methods [5], nucleotide-based detection [6], electrical impedance measurement [7], and matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight (MALDI-TOF) [8] have been developed to detect bacteria and the toxins in the modern food industry, however, the complex sample preparation, time-consuming as well as requiring professionals to operate cannot meet the needs of fast food safety testing. Therefore, there is necessary to establish a rapid, selective and sensitive method for the detection of pathogenic bacteria and toxins in foods.

ELISA is extensive used in food safety [9,10], disease diagnosis [11,12], and environmental protection [13,14]. Peroxidase serving as an important enzyme used in ELISA is vulnerable to various physical and chemical factors. As a consequence, artificial enzymes aiming to imitate the essential and general principles of natural enzymes with highly stable and low-cost attracted increasing attention in recent years [15,16]. Nanomaterials possess unique properties have been successively found to behave as peroxidase property, for example, gold nanoparticles [17,18], gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) [19,20], V2O5 nanowires [21], carbon nanomaterials [22–24]. Compared with natural enzymes, these peroxidase-like nanoparticles have the advantages of non-degradable, stable, insensitive to environmental changes and easy storage. Ultra-sensitively colorimetric assay for detection of targets by combining the peroxidase-like nanoparticles with ELISA was developed [25]. However, the tedious operation is a challenge of ELISA.

The development of nanocomposite structural materials with unique properties may be promising candidates for establishment of simple and rapid sensors [26,27]. Chitosan is one of the low cost and non-toxic natural biopolymers [28], which can also be used as an ideal solid support for the application of hybrid nanoparticles in different media [29,30]. Due to its excellent film-forming ability, biocompatibility and significant affinity with proteins, chitosan gained more attraction in immobilizing biomolecules, particularly enzymes [31,32]. The surface modification of chitosan membranes has opened up new avenues for immobilizing different enzymes and proteins for biomedical applications [33,34]. However, this membrane is affected by the activity of the natural enzyme always affected by the environment. Its alternative strategy is to use enzyme-like nanoparticles to develop stable and versatile chitosan-based biosensors.

In this study, fluorescent AuNCs were quickly prepared in the presence of chitosan and then peroxidase-like AuNCs were obtained from the as-prepared AuNCs under UV irradiation. Different from the early report [35] that chitosan–gold nanoparticles exhibited constant activity to catalyze the oxidation reaction of TMB, a novel strategy for colorimetric detection of target analytes is established based on the chitosan-AuNCs composite membrane, which the activity could be regulated by specific reactions associated with analyte, as shown in Scheme 1 . Combined with colored GNPs significantly amplifying the response signal affected by the exposed catalytic membrane depending on the amount of analytes, a highly selective and sensitive colorimetric readout for detection of S. aureus and SE-B was developed. Compared to the conventional ELISA, the proposed colorimetric strategy can be implemented in a facile and low-cost manner.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·3H2O), 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), BSA, chitosan and mercaptopropionic acid were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Staphylococcal enterotoxin B was received from Chinese Academy of Military Medical Sciences. DNA oligonucleotides were synthesized by Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co. (Shanghai, China). Their sequences are expressed as follows: The DNA aptamer against S. aureus: 5′-HS-TCCCTACGGCGCTAACCTCCCAACCGCTCCACCCTGCCTCCGCCTCGCCACCGTGCTACAAC-3′ [36]. The sequence of the SE-B aptamer was 5 -GGT ATT GAG GGT CGC ATC CAC TGG TCG TTG TTG TCT GTT GTC TGT TAT GTT GTT TCG TGA TGG CTC TAA CTC TCC TCT-3 [37]. All of the chemicals, unless mentioned otherwise, were of analytical reagent grade and obtained from the commercial source.

A simple reaction to synthesize highly fluorescent AuNCs was carried out according to the previous report [38]. First, 5 mg/mL of chitosan solution was prepared by dissolving chitosan in double distilled water containing 0.1% (v/v) acetic acid. After the filtration to remove the residual components, the pH of the solution was then adjusted to 6.4 by adding 10 M NaOH. Then 0.5 mL of resulting chitosan solution was added to 4 mL water. Subsequently, 40 μL of 0.11 M mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) was added to diluted chitosan solution under vigorous stirring conditions for 1 min. At last, 75 μL of 10 mM HAuCl4 was added dropwise to the mixture solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of peroxidase-like AuNCs by UV irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the peroxidase-like AuNCs, the as-prepared fluorescent AuNCs solution was kept irradiation under UV lamp (30 W) for 4 h. The peroxidase-like activity of UV-irradiated AuNCs was tested with the peroxidase-required substrate of TMB in the presence of H2O2. 500 μL of 5 mM TMB, 500 μL of 10 mM H2O2 and UV-irradiated AuNCs were added to 1 mL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.2). The solution was incubated at room temperature for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of peroxidase-like AuNC-Chitosan composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like AuNCs solution was added to 96-well micro titer plates, and then left it in vacuum oven to evaporate the solvent under 40 °C. The chitosan membrane formed on the micro-well was immerged in 0.1 mol/L NaOH solution, and then rinsed with plenty of distilled water repeatedly. The chitosan membrane that formed on the micro-wells was rinsed in 0.1 mol/L NaOH solution and plenty of distilled water repeatedly, respectively. The peroxidase-like activity of AuNC-chitosan membrane was tested as follow: 150 μL of 5 mM TMB, 150 μL of 10 mM H2O2 were added to micro-wells covered with AuNC-chitosan membrane. The solution was incubated at room temperature for 30 min.

Gram-positive bacterium S. aureus, B. subtilis and Gram-negative bacterium E.coli were incubated at 37 °C for 48 h. 300 μL of 0.004 mg/mL aptamer specific against S. aureus was added to micro-wells of plate for reacting 8 h. Then, a series of different concentrations of S. aureus, B. subtilis and E. coli (from 4 × 103 to 4 × 107 CFU/mL) were added to the micro-wells for 30 min. Subsequently, the micro-wells were washed with autoclaved NaCl solution to remove the excess bacteria on the micro-wells. To obtain colorimetric readout, 100 μL of 10 mM H2O2 was added, after 5 min, followed by the addition of 200 μL of reagents including 4 mM trisodium citrate, 22.4 μg/mL polyethylene glycol (200) and 1 mM chloroauric acid.

AuNC-chitosan membrane reacted with 350 μL, 0.004 mg/mL aptamer specific against SE-B at 4℃ overnight. The AuNC-chitosan membrane blocked with blocking buffer (350 μL of 1 mg/mL BSA solution) for 1 h at room temperature after washing the AuNC-chitosan membrane three times with wash buffer (0.01 M PBS buffer solution). Subsequently, 300 μL analyte was added. The AuNC-membrane was washed three times with wash buffer after 30 min, then, the reagents for displaying color were added.

To evaluate the applicability of AuNC-membrane, this colorimetric biosensor was evaluated the concentrations of SE-B mixed with different food samples, including corn, rice and flour. Foods of corn, rice and flour without SE-B were used as positive controls.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope at 80 kV. AFM measurements were carried out using Dimension Ion PT (Bruker). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using UV-1800 Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and the fluorescence intensity spectra were recorded by a RF-5301PC (Shimadzu, Japan) fluorescence spectrometer.

A simple and “one-step” reaction to synthesize highly fluorescent AuNCs was adopted [38], in which the AuNCs were obtained by the reaction of HAuCl4 and mercapto propionic acid (MPA) in the presence of chitosan at room temperature, and this chemical reaction can be completed within 15 min. Fig. 1 a depicts the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the generated colorless AuNCs does not show any peak in the region of 400–500 nm with the exception of a peak at 230 nm, however, a strong fluorescent emission peak at 583 nm under the excitation at 320 nm is found and indicative of the formation of AuNCs, excluding the formation of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) active GNPs, similar to the characteristic spectrum of AuNCs reported in the previous studies [39–41]. The morphology and size of the as-prepared AuNCs were characterized by TEM, which reveals the formation of spherical nanoscale particles with nearly uniform size distribution, as shown in Fig. 1b and c, displaying the diameter of the AuNCs is round 2 nm.

In fact, fluorescent AuNCs could be generated in just one minute of the reaction and the fluorescence intensity reached stable within 15 min. No change of the fluorescence spectrum of the as-prepared AuNCs was found over the time period of two weeks, indicating the as-prepared AuNCs in the solution are stable. Under UV irradiation, however, the fluorescence intensity of the AuNCs was gradually decreased even finally completely quenched after 4 h, as shown in Fig. 2 a. Meanwhile, the UV irradiation could lead to color variation of the corresponding solution from colorless to pale yellow depicted in Fig. 2b. Comparison of Figs. 2c with 1 a manifests that the absorption peak at 230 nm disappeared attributed to the formation of thiolated-Au after the initial AuNCs irradiated under UV light, suggesting the thiolated-Au was oxidized in this process. On the other hand, although the size of AuNCs irradiated under UV characterized by TEM (Fig. 2d) is the same with the initial AuNCs, the hydrodynamic size of AuNCs was reduced from 396 to 12 nm in diameter monitored by dynamic light scattering (DLS) after UV illumination. These phenomena seem that the AuNCs irradiated by UV has the same morphology and size as the initial AuNCs in addition to fluorescence.

Interestingly, peroxidase-like property might be induced after the as-prepared AuNCs irradiated by UV light. The peroxidase-like behavior of the UV-irradiated AuNCs was estimated using TMB as a chromogenic substrate, which would display blue color when it meets peroxidase [42,43]. Fig. 3 a shows that when addition of UV-irradiated AuNCs to solution of TMB and H2O2, color of the resulted solution changed from colorless to blue. The fact illustrates that the chromogenic reaction was stimulated by UV-irradiated AuNCs, otherwise, it couldn't be triggered. Factually, initial AuNCs generated from the reaction of HAuCl4 and MPA in the presence of chitosan did not catalyze the reaction of H2O2 oxidizing TMB, however, UV-irradiated AuNCs behave as peroxidase enzymes, whose fluorescence was completely quenched after UV illumination, can catalyze the reaction of H2O2 and TMB, displaying blue color of the oxidized TMB (Ox-TMB), as displayed in Fig. S1a. We speculate the peroxidase-like activity of UV irradiated-AuNC stems from the ability to produce
OH, which is the critical mediator responsible for the peroxidase-like activity. In the presence of TMB, the generated OH
radical quickly reacts with TMB to form ox-TMB displaying blue color. Otherwise, the OH
radicals interact each other to allow the decomposition of H2O2 to yield H2O and O2, as schematically illustrated in Fig. S1b. To obtain the best catalytic effect, the influence of UV irradiation time on the peroxidase-like activity was investigated. An intermediate reaction solution was extracted, and examined the catalytic capability using H2O2/TMB to display color associated with peroxidase-like activity, as shown in Fig. 3b, which displays color gradient from colorless to dark blue. It can be seen that the solution changed to light blue after 2 h UV irradiation, indicating the resulted AuNCs began to exhibit enzyme mimicking property. With the increase of UV irradiation the catalytic activity enhanced and then reached stability after 4 h. By carefully studying Fig. 3b, the alteration of the solution color has a certain relationship with the change of fluorescence intensity shown in Fig. 2a. It can be found that the peroxidase-like activity of AuNCs only appear after the fluorescence was completely quenched. Meanwhile, the absorbance of the produced ox-TMB catalyzed by irradiated AuNCs suggests the catalytic activity of the resultant AuNCs appears stable after 4 h UV irradiation; thus, we selected 4 h to be the UV irradiation time toward AuNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication colorimetric sensor based AuNC-chitosan composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The AuNC-chitosan composite membrane was fabricated on the 96-well micro titer plates. The membrane was characterized by AFM (Fig. 4 a) and the thickness of the membrane is about 60 μm. TEM was carried out to characterize the morphology of AuNC-chitosan composite membranes, as shown in Fig. 4b. The characterization of SEM presented in Fig. 4c reveals that the formed membrane was homogeneous. Fig. 4d manifests that AuNCs are predominantly spherical and well-dispersed without agglomeration in the membrane.

The peroxidase-like property of the AuNC-chitosan composite membrane was also explored, which was tested with TMB and H2O2. A comparison between AuNC-chitosan composite membrane and chitosan membrane presented in Fig. 5 a shows the AuNC-chitosan composite membrane catalyzing the reaction of H2O2 and TMB to form Ox-TMB, confirming its peroxidase-like property. In contrast, the H2O2/TMB reaction solution with chitosan membrane remained transparent, implying the lack of peroxidase-like activity. On the other hand, the catalytic activity of AuNC-chitosan composite membrane affected by thickness was investigated, as displayed in Fig. 5b, which manifests thicker film exhibits stronger catalytic activity. Except for AuNCs distributed on the membrane surface, the excellent permeability of heterogeneous composite membrane allowing the H2O2 to penetrate the membrane to react with AuNCs within the membrane. Thus, the thicker the AuNC-chitosan composite film, the stronger its catalytic activity. In this study, we choose the AuNC-chitosan membrane made from 1 mg/mL AuNC chitosan solution to carry out the following study.

A colorimetric assay for determination of targets was established based on specific reactions that occur on the peroxidase-like AuNC-chitosan composite membrane. The principle of the colorimetric sensor is illustrated in Scheme 1. The sensor is mainly composed of AuNC-chitosan composite membrane, aptamers against target analyte and H2O2, and the gold nanoparticles (GNPs) serving as output signal with varied colors depending on the amount of H2O2 were used for color indicator. Accordingly, a typical reaction of HAuCl4 and H2O2 to generate GNPs was introduced. As reported by previous works [19,20,25], sufficient amount of H2O2 reacts with HAuCl4 to produce well dispersed GNPs displaying wine red; however, insufficient H2O2 may form aggregated GNPs exhibiting blue or violet color. Thus, it is possibility that the colored GNPs produced from the reaction of H2O2 and HAuCl4 may indicate the catalytic activity of the composite membrane. An attempt to explain the feasibility of the catalytic AuNC-chitosan composite membrane was performed with experiment. Fig. 6 a manifests a significant decrease of H2O2 along with the reaction time in the presence of AuNC-chitosan composite membrane, however, the amount of H2O2 is not influenced by the chitosan membrane. Fig. 6b shows the corresponding color tonality obtained from the reaction of HAuCl4 and H2O2 in the presence of AuNC-chitosan composite membrane or chitosan membrane. The experimental results not only further confirm the peroxidase-like property of AuNC-chitosan composite membrane, but also demonstrate the feasibility of the colored GNPs serving as signal output in the designed colorimetric assay.

The key what we considered is to make a recognition event involving into a H2O2-based AuNPs-forming process, which can thus be disturbed by AuNC-chitosan composite membrane-catalyzed decomposition of H2O2. This consideration is easy to be realized in modulating catalytic activity induced by specific reactions between aptamer and target molecules. In the presence of target, the specific reaction-induced conjugate would mask the catalytic sites of AuNC-chitosan composite membrane, which leads to differing signal levels, that is, modulation of the signal associated with catalytic activity of the membrane, displaying various colored GNPs dependent on the amount of H2O2 affected by the number of target. On contrary, without target, H2O2 can easily reach the catalytic surface of composite membrane and the H2O2 subsequently decomposes to H2O and O2, enabling HAuCl4 to react with insufficient H2O2 to produce blue or violet aggregated GNPs. As a result, target can be directly detected by naked-eye readout through the composite membrane inducing color variation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiating bacteria and detection of staphylococcal enterotoxin B
ce:section.ce:para: Species differentiation is important for the early detection and identification of pathogenic and food-spoilage microorganisms that may be present food products. The main objective of this assay is to evaluate whether this colorimetric sensor could be used to identify and differentiated bacteria. Three kinds of bacteria including Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus, B. subtilis and Gram-negative E. coli were chosen to serve as model bacteria to evaluate the selectivity of this sensor. Firstly, the aptamer specific for S. aureus was immobilized on the AuNC-chitosan composite membrane coated on the micro-wells of the plate. Then, various concentrations (from 100 to 4 × 107 CFU/mL) of the three kinds of bacteria were added micro-wells for reaction of 30 min. The excess bacteria were removed and the micro-wells were washed with PBS solution, followed by addition of H2O2. Subsequently, the HAuCl4 was introduced to react with the remaining H2O2. As expected, colorimetric readout among the bacteria tested is shown in Fig. 7 a, in which S. aureus is distinguished from B. subtilis and E. coli, suggesting the recognition of bacteria with the unique composite membrane is feasibility. Furthermore, a color tonality from violet to wine red along with increasing concentration of S. aureus is appearance, which means various amounts of S. aureus could be detected depending on the catalytic membrane affected by the target bacteria. Obviously, high concentration of S. aureus forms aptamer-S. aureus structure might block more catalytic surface of the composite membrane, leading to its inability to decompose H2O2. This colorimetric assay based on specific recognition exhibits high sensitivity so that as lower as 1 × 102 CFU/mL S. aureus can be detected. Whereas, when this colorimetric sensor was used to detect various concentrations of other nonspecific bacteria, such as B. subtilis and E. coli, it displayed the same color as the blank and no distinguishable color was found among them. The results further confirmed the excellent selectivity of the sensing strategy for differentiating bacteria and detection of specific bacteria.

Considering that the recognition element of probe is replaceable once a new analyte is targeted, such a colorimetric readout sensing should be an extendable strategy or platform. The colorimetric strategy is extendable through a convenient way to sense a new analyte by use of a new capturing probe. Thus, we extended the sensing strategy just by using the specific probe responsible to sense SE-B secreted by S. aureus in the following study. SE-B is heat stable protein which may act as superantigen and allergen to induce symptoms of severe allergic and autoimmune diseases [44]. In addition, SE-B is considered as one of the most important virulence factor causing burn sepsis and staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome [45]. Similar to the determination of S. aureus, the aptamer against SE-B was functionalized on the AuNC-chitosan composite membrane and employed to capture SE-B. Different concentration of SE-B interact with the aptamer with varying efficiency in blocking catalytic membrane, leading to differing signal levels, that is, the modulation of the signal. Fig. 7b shows the color variation, from purple and red, is highly distinguishable by naked eyes and spectrometers as well, displaying a highly sensitive colorimetric assay. A distinguishable color differentiating SE-B from reference shows the limit of detection (LOD) for SE-B is 1.0 × 10−12 g/mL for naked-eye readout.

A certain concentration range of SE-B was investigated for further determination of SE-B, as shown in Fig. 8 a. In order to quantitatively detect the SE-B, the colorimetric readout was measured by absorption spectrometry. A linear relationship was found between the difference of absorbance (A = A0-A1) of GNPs and the concentrations of SE-B, c, in a range from 1 to 700 ng/mL, as shown in Fig. 8b. The calibration equation is A = 0.03601 + 9.245 × 10−5 c, with linear regression coefficient of 0.9959. As a practical application of the composite membrane-based sensor, food samples spiked with SE-B were tested. The spiked samples with different concentrations of SE-B were obtained by adding the SE-B into flour, corn and rice because the real samples cannot be available at present. The colorimetric readout from these samples were expectedly observed to display the red color depicted in Fig. 8c, and the accurate results measured by the proposed method were illustrated in Fig. 8d.

Conventional culture methods have been used for decades in detection of S. aureus[46]. However, time-consuming and complicated experimental procedures limit their application. In recent years, rapid detection methods for detection of S. aureus, such as polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [47], ELISA [48], and nucleic acid-based molecular biology methods [49], have been successfully developed, which significantly reduce the detection time from days to several hours. However, these advanced methods need extensive sample preparation and to be performed by trained personnel. Moreover, tedious procedures and possible bacterial contamination enable these methods not to be used as on-site detection methods.

Biosensors provide alternative strategy to perform microbial detection, allowing rapid on-site, real-time detection and identification of bacteria in food [50]. Meanwhile, numerous approaches including electrochemical [51], fluorescence [52], chemiluminescence [53], polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [54] and matrix-assisted laser-desorption/ionization-time of flight (MALDI-TOF) mass spectrometry [55] have been established to detect pathogenic bacteria or secreted enterotoxins. Generally, the bacteria or enterotoxins need to be isolated from food samples before detection; moreover, it is challenge to detect bacteria and secreted toxin in the food using the same method. The proposed colorimetric assay in this study will provide a platform to detect targets without isolation from food samples. Furthermore, both bacteria and the secreted toxins can be determined using the sensor by replacing the probe aptamer on the AuNC-chitosan membrane, allowing fast on-site, real-time and multiplex analysis, which is of great significance for the detection and identification of bacteria and toxins in foods.

In summary, a simple approach to prepare peroxidase-like AuNCs in the presence of chitosan induced by UV irradiation was developed. The AuNC-chitosan composite membrane also behaves peroxidase-like property and was characterized by TEM, AFM and SEM, which revealed that the formed membrane was homogeneous and the AuNCs are predominantly spherical and well-dispersed without agglomeration in the membrane. A colorimetric assay for determination of target was established by integrating immunoreactions occurred on the peroxidase-like AuNC-chitosan composite membrane with varying colored GNPs serving as output signal. The recognition element of probe anchored on the catalytic membrane is replaceable once a new analyte is targeted; therefore, such a colorimetric strategy is extendable. Three kinds of bacteria were chosen to serve as model bacteria to evaluate the selectivity of this sensor and S. aureus can be easily differentiated from E. coli and B. subtilis. This colorimetric assay can sensitively detect S. aureus as low as 4 × 102 CFU/mL and 1.0 × 10−12 g/mL SE-B for naked-eye readout, which provides promising platform for rapid onsite screening pathogenic bacteria and detection of enterotoxin in food safety and environmental monitoring.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely thank Prof. Guo from Chinese Academy of Military Medical Sciences to provide staphylococcal enterotoxin B. This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375036, 21675049, 21475040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.10.019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidase-like activity of polymer-coated cerium oxide nanoparticles

Clin. Infect. Dis.

Food Res. Int.

Compr. Rev. Food Sci. F.

Nat. Rev. Microbiol.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaoxue Xie received her bachelor's degree in 2017 and is currently a postgraduate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology. Her research interest is in the field of the development of bio/chem- sensors based on nanomaterials.

Fang Tan received her bachelor's degree in 2016 and is currently a postgraduate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology. Her research interest is in the field of the preparation of carbon-based nanomaterials and the development of bio/chem-sensors.

Aiqing Xu is an instructor in School of Life Science, Hunan University of Science and Technology. He received his doctor degree in Microbiology from Hunan Agricultural University in 2011. His current research interests include microbial resources development and application in the fields of fermented food production and safety.

Keqin Deng is currently a researcher and associate professor in Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Function Molecule, Ministry of Education and Hunan University of Science and Technology. He received his doctor degree from Institute of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University in 2010. His current research interests include novel nanomaterials and biosensing applications.

Yunlong Zeng is a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology. His research interests include fluorescent nanomaterials, electrochemical analysis, bio/chem-sensors.

Haowen Huang is a professor in Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Function Molecule, Ministry of Education and Hunan University of Science and Technology. He received his doctor degree from Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2004. His current research interests include nano-scale interfacial materials, development of nano-biosensor.
